ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific entry for plain fried yellow noodles; the broader 'noodle dish' class (FOODON_03315671) is used as the closest defensible parent anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00GJ (wheat noodles, cooked) is the closest defensible parent match; no specific code exists for stir-fried yellow wheat noodles in the FoodEx2 hierarchy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No sufficiently specific SNOMED CT concept found for plain fried yellow noodles; left null to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of plain fried yellow noodles as typically served at a Singaporean hawker centre weighs approximately 300 g.

nutrient_source.title: Plain Fried Yellow Noodles — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Plain Fried Yellow Noodles (F05060097). All per-100g values are sourced from this canonical entry with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — wheat noodles, yellow, cooked
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition, 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 58 (medium) is assigned based on the University of Sydney GI Database value for cooked yellow wheat noodles, which is the closest analogous food. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (58 × 68.1) / 100 ≈ 39.6. Serving carbohydrate = 22.7 g/100g × 300 g serving = 68.1 g. The input record cited GI 58 with GL 13.2 (per 100g basis); the per-serving GL here uses the 300 g standard plate.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 782 mg/100g — far exceeds the 600 mg/100g high-concern threshold, driven by soy sauce and dark soy sauce seasoning.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit plain fried yellow noodles; if consumed, request minimal soy sauce and restrict portion to no more than 150 g to keep sodium intake below 1,200 mg per meal.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Carbohydrate 22.7 g/100g with low dietary fibre (1.1 g/100g) from refined yellow wheat noodles produces a medium GI (58) and high glycemic load per standard serving (~39.6 GL units).
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size to 150–200 g, pair with non-starchy vegetables and lean protein to blunt the glycemic response, and monitor post-meal blood glucose given the high per-serving glycemic load.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very high sodium (782 mg/100g) raises blood pressure and cardiovascular risk; refined carbohydrate with low fibre contributes to dyslipidaemia risk.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should minimise consumption of plain fried yellow noodles due to the very high sodium content; if eaten, choose a small portion (≤150 g), request reduced soy sauce, and add vegetables to improve the fibre and micronutrient profile.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Sodium 782 mg/100g exceeds the 400 mg RA threshold, worsening tissue swelling; refined wheat noodles with low fibre (1.1 g/100g) drive pro-inflammatory glycemic load.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should significantly reduce soy sauce, add anti-inflammatory ingredients such as ginger and garlic, and include vegetables and lean protein to improve the overall anti-inflammatory nutritional profile.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Plain Fried Yellow Noodles (炒黄面; Mee goreng kuning kosong) is a Singaporean hawker staple consisting of fresh yellow wheat noodles stir-fried in a wok over high heat with soy sauce, dark soy sauce, garlic, and vegetable oil, without the addition of protein or vegetables in its plain form. The noodles are springy and slightly chewy with a savoury, mildly smoky flavour imparted by wok hei. Per 100 g, the dish provides 141 kcal, 4.5 g protein, 3.6 g fat, 22.7 g carbohydrate, 5.5 g sugar, 1.1 g dietary fibre, and a very high 782 mg sodium. The high sodium content — driven entirely by soy sauce and dark soy sauce — makes this dish unsuitable for individuals managing hypertension, cardiovascular disease, or rheumatoid arthritis without significant modification. The medium glycemic index (GI 58) combined with a high per-serving glycemic load (~39.6 for a 300 g plate) makes portion control essential for people with Type 2 Diabetes. The dish is commonly served as a hawker meal at breakfast, lunch, or dinner across Singapore and is widely available at food courts and coffee shops. It is vegetarian and vegan in its plain form but contains gluten (wheat) and soy allergens. Nutritional data sourced from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (F05060097).
common_user_queries: How many calories in plain fried yellow noodles?
Sodium content of mee goreng kuning
Can diabetics eat fried yellow noodles?
Is plain fried yellow noodles suitable for hypertension?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (F05060097) with confidence 0.95. This is an exact match from the primary approved regional source for Singaporean dishes. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for cooked yellow wheat noodles, which slightly reduces overall confidence but does not affect the high rating given the strong primary nutrient source.
notes: Fields set to -1 (data not available from HPB source): saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g. These detailed fatty acid, micronutrient, and moisture values are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for this dish (F05060097) as of 2026-04-16. Calcium (14.0 mg/100g) and iron (0.8 mg/100g) are reported by HPB and included. GI of 58 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for cooked yellow wheat noodles; the input record cited GI 58 from an 'Embedded GI Database' with low confidence (0.346), which has been replaced with the University of Sydney analogous estimate. Glycemic load per serving (39.6) is calculated for a 300 g standard plate. The input record incorrectly classified the cuisine as 'western'; this has been corrected to 'Singaporean' as plain fried yellow noodles is a canonical Singaporean hawker dish. The dietary tag 'low_calorie' from the input has been removed as 141 kcal/100g with a 300 g serving yields 423 kcal, which is not low-calorie for a main meal. Iteration 3 fix: ontology.foodon_match_type changed from 'broad' to 'closest_parent' and ontology.foodex2_match_type changed from 'adapted' to 'closest_parent' to conform to the allowed values ['closest_parent', 'exact', 'none'] required by the validator.
